text:
I dedicate this article to Professor George Lotter, who has supervised in excess of ninety postgraduate students in Practical Theology at the North-West University (NWU). He has played a seminal role in a diverse body of empirical work, a development that reflects the worldwide and local increase in empirical approaches in Practical Theology. An essential contribution to the empowerment of lecturers and students who must utilise these approaches on a regular basis is still required. This article urges that greater sophistication may be achieved if greater control and direction is achieved in particular stages of qualitative research, specifically during the processes of coding and data analysis. Consequently, the author develops a structured approach to coding and data analysis which could enrich the research enterprise in Practical Theology.

Die article is dedicated to prof. George Lotter who supervised more than 90 postgraduate students in Practical Theology at the North-West University (NWU). Under his leadership a substantial body of empirical research nationally and internationally adhered to the empirical orientation common to the discipline. It is imperative for lecturers and students to reflect on their competence in empirical research methodologies. In this article, an argument is made for a more systematic approach to coding and analysing data in qualitative research, and a suggested model is given for application in practical theology.
